text: Mr. Lee Roy Moore and you were talking about the 1944-1949 you could sure leak and you were talking about the Cambridge Dodgers and Grand Prix. So what really happened was by Branch Ricky coming forth with the support for the Cambridge Dodgers and the rest of the major league teams kind of fell in line and then we had the Eastern Shore League of 1946-49 and this was a very prominent time here and they did very well. We had the ballpark was out with the soccer center is now and they were called the Salisbury Cardinals. Of course there was a Cambridge team and the Eastern Biltbert was in it, Cepert was in it. I remember going to some of those games because I remember seeing Frank Malzone playing and he went to the Red Sox later and Mel Barnell was with centerville left-handed pitcher and he went with the Red Sox and of course the Norm Zachin set all kinds of records and Steve Bilko was here and Bilko played for the Dodgers and Zach and played Red Sox with them. Which team do you remember was one? Was the Red Sox on the board or they just pulled players? Well Melford was a Red Sox on the board. Melford was a Red Sox on the board team and Cambridge was a Dodger. Your Salisbury was a Cardinals as Cepert was you know I'm not really sure. You got so many you get a big up and go into the wall. But they were all affiliated with a major league team and they were paying the players and the crowd was pretty good. What happened in the 49th that the league stopped in 49th? Well I've always said that one of the reasons why that it folded was lack of attendance was a great thing because at that but period of time we had other things going on in the area. You know there was a television was coming on the scene. I don't know the attendance. Money wasn't good with it at time either. All the baseball was there. I think it may be softball and something to do with it too. We started to come into popularity about 1950s. Look at your fan. That's picture. That's where some of us old baseball players sort of went for a while. In terms of they sort of tried to get baseball where they owned. There are still some there's some late 20th century and 21st century for some of us over general. You know you don't mean like over to Hill Gang or what on the wings that I saw in art was called the Pugs Daily Home Creek. So you know that goes back. But anyhow I think that the city was not supporting the team like it should. Did they have the tenants who were really bad at it? Did the African Americans support baseball all in town? Did it resolve their team? I think to a certain degree that they did. Yeah they had their own teams. Yeah they had their own teams I was just wondering. They were working with the games. Well some of them did certainly. But of course then on weekends they had their own schedules to play also. So it took away from the tenants which I think was a big factor. It's still like playing the games. So you probably had to get sort of out fishing heads to white leagues, the African Americans leagues. And then you had the minor league teams and people had to pay to go those where you want to watch these. And then the first one in drama is plus you probably knew some of the people there. So you had another attack. Yeah so it was interesting. So now we have the soul group in part of the Sally League since 1996. 1996 yes. And of course we came in with the Montreal team because the Orioles couldn't furnish a team at that time. But then in 1997 we had been affiliated with the Orioles over cents. We'll get different types of baseball today. Yes, we have interesting that we're getting all subject here. The three Sally League goes down to... They didn't go down to Georgia. They go down to the Bay Plate teams in Georgia. And I know West Virginia and North Carolina. Now we've got some new teams. We have one in Ohio and we've got one in Tennessee and one in Ohio and one in New Jersey. We have three new teams. That's a lot of expense. You know this guy's... They're riding that gray now somewhere and it's sitting in that bus greater than an hour and that's really real. And they have good quality baseball come out. But they're riding in a much better motor transportation than I did when I played ball with North Carolina. But we just rode in an old bus. Old school bus. But these guys got her air conditioning and good seats and probably a way to scratch out. And of course they've got their little toys like their sound machines and their video games. And so a proof plug in. It's stuck to pass them to the time of death. We've got a couple questions here in relation to the African Americans. As we all know, Jackie Robinson came into baseball with the proof plug daughters as you mentioned before and ran tricky. And I feel Jackie Robinson changed the face of baseball because he brought in some people who have been elected. So you did. Your comments on that? Well, I think that when you look back at history with how the African Americans were treated and were heavy, it wasn't habitable that this was going to come about. It was just not a matter of how but when. And of course they did happen. And this broke loose the opportunity for some of the real good African Americans to get into the major leagues. And I mean, look at, you know, I mean, the sexual page. I mean, I'm pitching it. I forget what age it was for some years old, you know. And one of the things I'd like to mention right here is what William Maye says a great saying about his career. He says, every time he says, I get a paycheck. He says, I thank Jackie Robinson. He says he was he saying he opened the door. Yeah. You know, Mr. William Miles who played with the Oakville Eagles to me and his interview that Jackie Robinson was not that good of all player from the Negro League. He said that he was somebody that could have such a great personality and could put up with all the abuses and everything for an tricky picking. There was always guys that would be greater kidders, greater field builders and more dynamic. But Jackie Robinson was the first one at times and he, him and the Volary Delby, failed place. True. And they had some great players. I mean, Josh Gibson and all those guys, I mean, never just good. I mean, and of course there's a lot of history that they have. We've got some books on them and what have you. And they had some great teams, a great, a lot of art. And of course, you know, we've got Judy Johnson and Good Morning Snow Hill. And what's going to be asked about Mr. Johnson when I started doing this investigation of these two short baseballs. And then that Judy William Julius Johnson was from Snow Hill. And he's a member of baseball, Paul Hanks, the sixth from the Negro League. Any information we're talking about Mr. Johnson? Well, he was the first one to go into Coopers Town, there's a black player in which Jamal stood. He played third base for, I forget how many years for the Grey's and the Monarch and a couple of the teams. And then he later, not only managed, but became a scout also for a couple of teams. So he was very well at first and had the knowledge of the game to be able to accomplish what he did. You're exhibiting him, Paul there, has a great article by the Wilmington, who's taken the journal, named him the Sportsman of the Century. And it's told about how a great ball player he was and I think Tony Mac said his only problem was he was born. If he had been white, he could have named him Christ and one of the true Johnson, he said that he was born too soon. And they said that the combat that he made, I think, was an interview, was it? He was a teacher of the game. And they're teaching him in the game and helping put baseball in the map. That's cool. We have some really great people. And of course there was some many great American players who still are. About the Seabird Hawks. You have a big display of the Hawks. I've got to take a picture of them. Let me tell you a little bit about the Seabird Hawks, how they came about as a team. It was a fellow by the name of Rubin Collins, who was in World War II. And when he came out of the service and back to his hometown of Seabird, and he seemed what was going on and it wasn't enough interest with some of the young players to do things. He said, we need to form a baseball team. That's what he did. He went to work and formed a baseball team to see for Hawks. Another man that doesn't live here now in this area, but lives across the Bay Bridge. And his name is... I got it right here in some place. Anyhow, it is to make any numbers. He backed Rubin into helping get his team together. And there has been five of them that have been nominated to be inducted into this hall of fame. But we've only inducted one so far. And that's Rubin Collins, the man who organized, but they were one of the better black teams in this area. Against the folks Bill Legels and the Queen City Tigers. And of course, a lot of the team and some of the other ones had black teams up. I've seen a couple of them play. What do you remember about? Very much alive all the time. A quick reflex is good. Desire to play. You think they're desire? But, again, at the same time, enjoying themselves. A lot of people take things too serious sometimes in them and enjoy the game. Like, I think they should. Well, I noticed Bill, both the Levy Bulls and Sam Downs. No, no, no. And also, the down is on the board of directors here and the other teams here. And from the information I had, he was sort of like the... I was, yes, off the bat. He was kind of that way. He was a one that organized the Elksville Eagles. It was true and made them into the team to be reckoned with. And what's another thing that's interesting about the Elksville Eagles is they knew the white players too, being all of them. Because sometimes they play each other. But if they were short, a couple of players, sometimes, in that play again, they would grab a couple of guys from the white teams to fill in. So they would have enough players. And I talked to a couple of them and I said, hey, they were just normal guys. They just never had any problem playing well at all. Like, playing game, but integration in this area didn't really take place until the 60s. And it was a trouble period from all the invitations. I know it was being them. Not being the area. There certainly was some differences in the adventure for sure. And that's been worked out. Yeah. And there was another... That was eventually going to happen too. Yeah. But the Queen City Tiger was another good team. And of course, Mr. Dome was responsible for some of that. He, of course, is deceased now, but he served on board of directors, the original board of directors and was with us for many, many years. And was instrumental in getting some of the players conducted and do the follow-up, and the deserve to be here. I noticed that you've been adopted. Yeah. Yeah. You know, I feel very grateful that I was. It's a special feeling. It's going to be inducted into... I look at it this way. And we look at all the other players that I see inducted. I feel as though it was a lot better than I was. Okay. And just good to be with this group. And of course, I have an uncle. Two uncles that's in here in the cousin. That played a minor league ball and uncle played major league ball. And then... I come from a whole playing family. And that's... A lot of people don't realize that that part of the teaching was done. You tagged along with the brothers and they taught you the right way. And they probably threw at you and picked on you and did that. I remember going to baseball games at Prince's Ann with my dad when I was, you know, just very small. But I was impressed by setting there and watching these players play on Sundays and just seen to be enjoying yourself. And yet they were very good to me. So today, you don't see that, of course, you have camps and things like this work. And some go out and play, sort of on your own like in the old days. You have so many martyrs and things like that. So, you know, there's a lot to take them away from them. Other actors that they have, you know, the Citroën. Some of the people I have to know here, I mentioned on here, people like Bill Miles, George Stewart, Kurt Moll, Dezongel. Yes, some of those. Yes, he looked at those people. Because in our work, I met these people. You know, on the O2A Eagles and I suspect they were all good, yeah. And Mr. Downcoach, then, I guess. Oh, yeah. He was very, a lot of influence he had on those individuals. Sam was very personal. I think that's what I heard. We're sorry to our little bit more supporters here. I'm going to just feel it's about baseball and it's a theft of your life. That's a problem. Definitely has made my life more enjoyable when you can play at something that you really like and you have a labor to love for. You're going to do it better, play it better, feel good about it. And then you can see the results. But one of the things that I really think back on is I helped coach and manage teams in later in my life. And I could see where I had influence on players too by knowing what I had experienced. And this made me feel good knowing that I've helped them improve themselves. And I think that's what it's all about is these guys are that form these little league teams years ago and what heavy it is. And they deserve a lot of credit for getting the youth involved keeping them off the streets, getting in trouble and stuff, having them learn something. Some of them eventually went to the minor and major leagues. So, yes, it was a good experience. What do you think about baseball and that? We touched on that in between talking and getting a big smile on your face and I know we could go. Go ahead. Well, I look at baseball today. I don't take away from the players because there's a lot of exceptionally good players today. I mean, really good players. But baseball today has got two things that I think hurt a lot of it. It's entertainment and there's a lot of money. And I think it takes away from some of the players' ability to improve yourself from one year to the next. In other words, they don't have an incentive. I've always said that if a player bats 305 this particular year and he gets a raise, say $10,000 raise. And then in the next year, only bats 275, then he didn't deserve a raise. He's got to improve that he can get better than that three, whatever it was he vetted in order to move up. I may say we would have pet her if he's got a low earner in the average paying him a courting rate. But now a lot of them know that they're going to be making $11,000, $12 million a year. So, I don't know. The incentive has been taken away. But don't get me wrong, we should still have a lot of big boulder. What's your feelings about Mark McWire, very bond breaking in the Davis and Roger Maris's record? I would say probably very bond is a little bit of chance to break Davis's 715 and Hank Aaron's 715. If they keep the civil side for play. Well, I think this was eventually going to happen when you look at the big picture. A lot of people don't know or don't think about it. Even though Aaron broke the big Bruce Retard, it took him longer to do it. More bats, more of this. So, if you put it in perspective, you know, the big Bruce was put in the baseball in the map. He was a personable figure and everybody liked him and he did great things for baseball. What sort of Aaron, what sort of boy are you? Bones and those guys, and I think Bones will break the record. How are you going to stop me? McWire are the same way. Records are made to be broken. Except maybe one might not be broken. 10 Williams is betting average over 400. That's going to be hard. How about Kal's Game Street? Nobody thought it would be broken either. Maybe Joe DeVonju is hitting three. It's very in right thing. You see him has a display on somebody who I thought was a great ball player. I still think he's a great ball player, but he got himself into some problems. And that's B Rose. And if you feel like you want B Rose, and you just play out there, so I like should he be in the hall of the game? If you was to go just by his statistics, and his playing ability and betting average isn't run, then we have to get into another situation as to what he did. And I've always said that he just happened to be one of the ones that got caught. Who knows? He has a tendency to deal with their care. Sometimes if you're arrogant and rude, people are looking out for it. What would you say? Baseball was the score of the Eastern Shore until the 60s and then all of a sudden he went in. 59 when the Central Shore League folded. So the ball took over. We're able to talk about that. That's all. And I mean this is at a time when television was really going strong, other avenues of entertainment. A lot of people were able to afford boats and were going boating when Sunday, fishing. And the players from World War II, they came back and they were getting to an age where they were sometimes ready to hang it up. And I know it's the best it's all ball. We're going to go through different faces. That's one of the things that's working on this work. It's that we do have an Eastern Shore League that's semi-core weight and there's some good ball players out there. And it struggles. Some of the baseball is still a lot, but I guess now we get to the soccer out there and football. Across the ball and basketball and tennis. I really like to thank you very much. It's been very entertaining for me to be through kind of a machete right here in Washington. I hope that it has a good A.C.V. which you are a wealth of knowledge and certainly that that sort of people will have. Then my pleasure, I mean anytime that you can talk baseball if you love it like I do that's an asset you know that you have and the only thing that I would like to see is more people that come to the game would come to the museum because a lot of people don't even know that we're here and they just walk by the door and up into the elevator and out to the bad people. But we are getting more exposure and I appreciate you doing this because this is going to help us and we've got more people are already articles in the paper and this helps. The NAMP Center who was all very university has a very colorful display about what you have in the museum here and a bunch of people will talk to me about it and I've already seen it and that was sort of the reason I came over here. Snooping, we're investigating and we're right into you. So, I thank you, Dan sir. I appreciate you coming by and Mike and anytime I can help you I just let me know. Alright, I'll see you.
